title: Greetings: Meeting Someone for the First Time
objectives: Greet someone politely when meeting for the first time.
Choose between formal and informal greetings.
Recognize natural Mexican and Spain Spanish greeting variations.
Introduce yourself with a simple phrase.
Respond politely when someone greets you.

vocabulary.tip: The safest greeting in almost any situation.
Use from morning until around midday.
Use after midday and before evening.
Use as a greeting at night or as a goodbye before bed.
Use after learning someone's name.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Nice to meet you, my name is Tom.

vocabulary.tip: Use encantado if you identify as male, encantada if female.
Informal; use with people your age, friends, and family.
Formal; use with strangers, elders, or in professional settings.
A short, common answer to ¿cómo estás?
A warmer answer than bien.
Use when you are okay but not great.
Informal way to return the question.
Formal way to return the question.
Literally means I call myself.
Use soy for identity, origin, and profession.
Use de for origin.
Essential politeness word.
A warmer thank you.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Thank you very much for the help.

vocabulary.tip: The standard response to gracias.
Often goes at the end of a request.
Use for small interruptions or apologies.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Excuse me, what is your name?

vocabulary.tip: Use when passing by or entering/leaving a space.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Excuse me, I am going to pass.

vocabulary.tip: Use for apologies with more emotional weight.
Formal and useful with strangers.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Excuse me, do you speak English?

vocabulary.tip: Common, friendly goodbye.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: See you later, nice to meet you.

greetings.whenUsed: Use hola everywhere. ¿Qué onda? is casual in Mexico; ¿qué tal? is very common in Spain and broadly understood.
Use in the morning. Mexican ¿cómo amaneciste? asks how someone woke up and feels warm or familiar.
Use after midday. Buenas is a shortened casual greeting common in many regions.
Use at night. It can be a greeting or a farewell.
Use with friends, peers, and family. ¿Qué tal? is shorter and very natural.
Use with strangers, elders, clients, or anyone you want to address respectfully.
Use immediately after introductions. Encantado/encantada changes with speaker gender.
Me llamo is clear and beginner-friendly. Soy is shorter and very natural in introductions.
Use after answering a greeting. Use usted instead of tú in formal situations.
Adiós is final. Nos vemos and hasta luego feel more like see you later.
Use when you expect to see the person the next day.
Casual only. Avoid with formal contacts or first meetings unless the tone is relaxed.
Use when receiving someone. Bienvenido changes to bienvenida for a woman.
Use formal conocerle/conocerlo with respect. Conocerte is informal.
Use after the first hello to sound more conversational.

grammar.topic: Greeting politely and choosing formal or informal speech
grammar.explanation: Spanish greetings depend on time of day, relationship, and level of respect. Use tú forms with friends, peers, and family. Use usted forms with strangers, elders, clients, or anyone you want to address politely.

notes.title: Tú vs usted
notes.text: Use tú with people you know well. Use usted when you want to sound respectful or professional.

notes.title: Me llamo vs soy
notes.text: Me llamo is clear and works in any introduction. Soy plus your name is shorter and common in casual introductions.

notes.title: Buenos vs buenas
notes.text: Use buenos with días: buenos días. Use buenas with tardes and noches: buenas tardes, buenas noches.

notes.title: Encantado / encantada
notes.text: Use encantado if the speaker identifies as male and encantada if the speaker identifies as female. The word describes the speaker, not the person being greeted.

notes.title: Returning the question
notes.text: After answering ¿cómo estás? or ¿cómo está?, add ¿y tú? or ¿y usted? to keep the exchange polite and natural.

examples.english: Hello, my name is Tom. And you?
Nice to meet you, I am Ana.

grammar.practicePrompt: Introduce yourself once to a friend and once to an older family member.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Good morning. My name is Carlos.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: Nice to meet you, my name is Tom.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: Very well, thank you. And you?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Excuse me, what is your name?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: My name is Tom. Nice to meet you.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Hola, soy Tom. Soy de Canadá.
lines.english: Hi, I'm Tom. I'm from Canada.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Hi, how's it going? How is everything?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: Thank you very much, nice to meet you.

pronunciation.focus: Clear vowels, silent h, Spanish ñ, and polite greeting rhythm.
tips: The h is silent in hola and hasta. Start with the vowel sound: ola, asta.
Keep Spanish vowels short and steady. Do not turn hola into hoh-luh.
The ñ in mañana sounds like ny in canyon: ma-NYA-na.
In most of Latin America, gracias uses an s sound: GRA-sias.
In much of Spain, gracias may use a th sound: GRA-thias.
In ¿cómo está?, stress the final tá. In ¿cómo estás?, stress tás.
For polite requests, slow down slightly: Perdón... ¿puede repetir?

items.note: Do not pronounce the h: ola.
Stress dí in días; keep buenos smooth.
Keep the final s light but audible.
Say both words clearly; do not drop buenas.
The u in gusto is pronounced: GUS-to.
Informal; keep the final s audible.
Pause slightly after más for clarity.

listeningPhrases.english: Good morning, nice to meet you.

culture.title: Greeting with warmth and respect
culture.text: A good Spanish greeting is usually more than one word. In a first meeting, use a greeting plus mucho gusto and choose usted if the person is older, a client, or someone you want to respect. With friends or people your age, hola and ¿qué tal? sound natural. In family settings, people may greet warmly with a handshake, hug, or cheek kiss depending on the country and closeness. When unsure, start politely and copy the other person’s level of formality.

nativeSpeech.title: How native speakers really greet
notes: Many speakers shorten buenas tardes or buenas noches to buenas in casual settings.
¿Qué tal? is one of the safest natural greetings across Spain and much of Latin America.
In Mexico, ¿qué onda? is friendly and casual, but it is not for formal introductions.
Native speakers often stack phrases: Hola, ¿qué tal? Mucho gusto.
Bien, gracias. ¿Y tú? is simple, polite, and useful when you are just starting.
With older relatives or professional contacts, ¿cómo está? sounds warmer and safer than ¿cómo estás?

commonMistakes.correction: Use ¿cómo está? in formal or respectful situations.
commonMistakes.explanation: The formal version is safer with strangers, elders, and people you have just met.

commonMistakes.mistake: Pronouncing the h in hola or hasta.
Using adiós when you expect to see someone soon.
commonMistakes.correction: Use hasta luego or nos vemos.
commonMistakes.explanation: Adiós can sound more final than see you later.
The fixed greeting is plural: buenos días.

commonMistakes.correction: Say bien, gracias. ¿Y tú? or ¿y usted?
commonMistakes.explanation: Returning the question makes the greeting feel complete and polite.

quiz.prompt: Choose the formal way to ask “How are you?”
Which phrase is the formal “And you?”
You are meeting your partner’s grandmother. Choose the safest greeting.
Choose the natural casual Mexican greeting.
Choose the natural Spain Spanish greeting for “How’s it going?”
Choose the Spanish for “My name is Tom.”
A friend says: Hola, ¿qué tal? Choose a natural reply.
Someone speaks too fast. What should you say?

speakingChallenge.prompt: Carlos has just introduced himself. Greet him, say your name, and say nice to meet you.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Hola, Carlos. Me llamo Tom. Mucho gusto.

speakingChallenge.prompt: You are meeting an older family member for the first time. Use a formal greeting and return the question politely.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Buenas tardes. Muy bien, gracias. ¿Y usted?

speakingChallenge.prompt: A friend your age says “Hola, ¿qué tal?” Answer naturally and ask “and you?”
Carlos speaks too quickly. Ask him politely to repeat more slowly.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Perdón, ¿puede repetir más despacio, por favor?

speakingChallenge.prompt: Introduce yourself to your spouse’s family using one time-of-day greeting, your name, and one polite expression.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Buenas tardes, me llamo Tom. Mucho gusto.

realLifeMission.title: Real Life Mission
realLifeMission.mission: Introduce yourself to Carlos using at least three greetings and one polite expression.
successCriteria: Start with one time-of-day greeting, such as buenos días, buenas tardes, or buenas noches.
Use at least two additional greetings or introduction phrases from the lesson.
Include at least one polite expression, such as por favor, gracias, mucho gusto, or perdón.
End naturally with a short closing, such as hasta luego or nos vemos.
